prompt: The following table shows pairwise KING-robust kinship coefficients and IBS0 statistics for six rice accession pairs from the 3K Rice Genomes Project. Using the standard KING thresholds (Duplicate/MZ twin: >0.354, First-degree: 0.177-0.354, Second-degree: 0.0884-0.177, Third-degree: 0.0442-0.0884, Unrelated: <0.0442), classify each pair's relationship degree. Comment on any notable differences in IBS0 between pairs in the same relationship category.
context:
KING-robust kinship estimates for six rice accession pairs:

Pair | Sample_A         | Sample_B         | KINSHIP | IBS0   | Subpopulations
-----|------------------|------------------|---------|--------|------------------
1    | IRIS_313-10177   | IRIS_313-10421   | 0.290   | 0.00312| Both temp-japonica
2    | IRIS_313-8695    | IRIS_313-9102    | 0.246   | 0.01805| Both indica
3    | IRIS_313-10893   | IRIS_313-11025   | 0.102   | 0.01847| Both temp-japonica
4    | IRIS_313-8695    | IRIS_313-10177   | -0.042  | 0.112  | Cross-subpop (ind x tej)
5    | IRIS_313-9318    | IRIS_313-10421   | -0.018  | 0.098  | Cross-subpop (ind x tej)
6    | IRIS_313-11025   | IRIS_313-10177   | 0.114   | 0.02017| Both temp-japonica

All samples genotyped by WGS at ~14x depth. 205,418 LD-pruned biallelic SNPs used.
correct_answer: Pair 1 (kinship=0.290): First-degree (0.177-0.354). Pair 2 (kinship=0.246): First-degree (0.177-0.354). Pair 3 (kinship=0.102): Second-degree (0.0884-0.177). Pair 4 (kinship=-0.042): Unrelated (<0.0442). Pair 5 (kinship=-0.018): Unrelated (<0.0442). Pair 6 (kinship=0.114): Second-degree (0.0884-0.177). Notably, pairs 1 and 2 are both classified as first-degree, but pair 1 (both temperate japonica) has a much lower IBS0 (0.00312) compared to pair 2 (both indica, IBS0=0.01805). This likely reflects the higher homozygosity in temperate japonica (a more bottlenecked subpopulation with higher selfing), which naturally reduces the number of opposite-homozygote sites (IBS0) even at the same kinship level. The negative kinship values in cross-subpopulation pairs (4, 5) are expected -- KING-robust estimates kinship relative to the sample average, so pairs from highly differentiated subpopulations (indica vs. temperate japonica) share fewer alleles than average, producing negative estimates.
rubric: Must correctly classify all six pairs into the appropriate KING threshold categories. Must note the IBS0 discrepancy between pairs 1 and 2 despite both being first-degree, and attribute it to subpopulation differences (homozygosity levels) rather than a biological difference in relatedness type. Must correctly handle negative kinship (pairs 4, 5) as unrelated due to cross-subpopulation comparison. Major errors: misclassifying any pair's relationship degree, interpreting negative kinship as an error, or failing to note the IBS0 difference.
notes: In rice, the high selfing rate produces very high homozygosity which compresses IBS0 values. Cross-subpopulation pairs between indica and japonica will show negative KING kinship due to strong population structure.
